stem:
A 9-year-old boy with a diagnosis of asthma attends a routine review at the general practice where you work as a registrar. He has been on budesonide-formoterol 100/6 micrograms two inhalations twice daily as maintenance for the past 12 months. He reports no daytime symptoms over the past three months, no night waking, and full participation in school sport without pre-exercise reliever.

His mother is keen to reduce his medication burden. He has had no oral corticosteroid courses in the past 18 months and no hospital presentations. His inhaler technique with a spacer is excellent and adherence has been confirmed by pharmacy refill data.

data_table.Examination: Weight 30 kg, chest clear, no nasal polyps, no eczema flare
data_table.Investigations: Office spirometry FEV1 105% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.88, bronchodilator response negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in his asthma plan?

options.text: Switch to fluticasone propionate 250 micrograms twice daily and review symptoms in 3 months
options.rationale: Switching to a higher-equivalent-dose ICS as a single agent escalates total ICS exposure and is the opposite of step-down in a child with sustained control. Fluticasone 250 micrograms twice daily is a high paediatric dose by Australian conventions. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook - Children)

options.text: Continue current dose unchanged for a further 6 months before reassessing step-down options
options.rationale: Prolonged continuation of an ICS-LABA in a child with sustained excellent control unnecessarily exposes him to growth velocity effects and adrenal suppression risk. After 3 months of stable control, step-down should be actively trialled rather than deferred. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook - Children)

options.text: Cease budesonide-formoterol and prescribe salbutamol 100 micrograms as a reliever only when needed
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of preventer therapy in a child with confirmed asthma risks loss of control and exacerbation. SABA-only therapy is no longer recommended for any asthma above intermittent severity given the mortality signal in studies such as SABINA. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook - Children)

options.text: Switch to montelukast 5 mg orally once daily as maintenance monotherapy and stop inhaled therapy
options.rationale: Montelukast is less effective than ICS for paediatric asthma control and carries a TGA boxed warning for neuropsychiatric adverse effects. It is not a recommended step-down from a well-tolerated ICS-LABA in a child with confirmed responsive disease. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook - Children)

options.text: Reduce maintenance to budesonide-formoterol 100/6 micrograms one inhalation twice daily and review in 3 months
options.rationale: After at least 3 months of well-controlled asthma on a low-dose ICS-LABA, step-down by approximately 50% is appropriate, with review at 2 to 3 months to confirm sustained control. Halving the daily ICS dose while maintaining twice-daily dosing is the recommended step-down for paediatric stable asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook - Children)

explanation.summary: Paediatric asthma maintenance should be stepped down once control has been sustained for at least 3 months on the current regimen. The typical approach is to halve the ICS dose while preserving the regimen architecture, then reassess at 2 to 3 months. Complete cessation of preventer therapy is reserved for children who have remained on the lowest dose with excellent control for at least 12 months. Spirometry, symptom diaries, and reliever frequency are all useful objective markers when titrating.
key_points: Step-down trialled after at least 3 months of sustained well-controlled asthma
Halve ICS dose rather than removing LABA partner first when on combination
Review at 2 to 3 months with spirometry and symptom assessment
Maintain a written asthma action plan throughout any step-down
explanation.pearls: In a child on low-dose ICS-LABA with sustained control for at least 3 months, the recommended step-down is to halve the ICS-LABA dose while keeping twice-daily dosing intact, then reassess at 3 months before considering further reduction.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - Asthma in Children
BMJ Best Practice - Asthma in Children

stem: An 8-year-old girl is brought to the paediatric emergency department where you are the registrar with two weeks of polyuria, polydipsia, and 3 kg weight loss. Today she has been vomiting and is drowsy but rousable. Capillary blood glucose reads HIGH on the ward meter. Capillary ketones are 4.8 mmol/L. She weighs 26 kg. Her father describes her as alert but exhausted. She has no significant past history and takes no medications.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130 bpm, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 32 deep and sighing, Temp 36.5 degrees C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Mucous membranes dry, capillary refill 3 seconds, GCS 14 (E4 V4 M6)
data_table.Investigations: Venous pH 7.18, bicarbonate 9 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.9 mmol/L, glucose 32 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial fluid management in the first hour?

options.text: Sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg intravenously over 30 minutes to correct the acidosis before any further fluid
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not recommended in paediatric DKA except in life-threatening hyperkalaemia or pH below 6.9 with cardiovascular compromise. It paradoxically worsens intracellular acidosis and increases cerebral oedema risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Sodium chloride 0.9% as a 20 mL/kg bolus over 15 minutes, then commence insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour
options.rationale: A 20 mL/kg rapid bolus exceeds the cautious volume recommended for paediatric DKA and is associated with increased cerebral oedema risk in this age group. Insulin is also typically commenced 1 to 2 hours after fluids are started, not concurrently with the initial bolus. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Hartmann's solution at maintenance rate alone, without an initial bolus, while awaiting electrolyte results
options.rationale: A clinically dehydrated child with venous pH 7.18 and tachycardia requires an initial resuscitation bolus, not maintenance-only fluid. Waiting for further electrolytes to act would worsen her acidosis and perfusion. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Sodium chloride 0.45% with 5% dextrose and 40 mmol/L potassium chloride at 1.5 times maintenance from the outset
options.rationale: Half-normal saline with dextrose is the typical rehydration fluid AFTER the initial resuscitation bolus and once glucose has fallen, not the first-hour resuscitation choice. Starting dextrose immediately at glucose 32 mmol/L is also inappropriate. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Sodium chloride 0.9% as a 10 mL/kg bolus over 30 to 60 minutes, then reassess perfusion before further fluids
options.rationale: Australian paediatric DKA guidance recommends a cautious initial resuscitation bolus of 10 mL/kg of 0.9% sodium chloride over 30 to 60 minutes for a child with clinical dehydration but without shock, given the risk of cerebral oedema with aggressive fluid loading. Perfusion is reassessed before any further bolus. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

explanation.summary: Initial management of paediatric DKA in Australia favours cautious fluid resuscitation due to the well-documented risk of cerebral oedema. A 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride over 30 to 60 minutes is the standard initial step when there is clinical dehydration without shock. Insulin is commenced at 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/hour around 1 to 2 hours after fluids begin to avoid rapid glucose drops. Potassium is added to ongoing fluids once urine output is established and serum potassium is known.
key_points: Initial bolus 10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride over 30 to 60 minutes in non-shocked paediatric DKA
Insulin infusion commenced 1 to 2 hours after fluids at 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/hour
Bicarbonate restricted to life-threatening hyperkalaemia or pH below 6.9 with shock
Cerebral oedema risk peaks 4 to 12 hours into treatment, especially in new-onset diabetes under 5 years
explanation.pearls: In paediatric DKA the initial 10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride bolus is given over 30 to 60 minutes, not as a rapid push, and insulin is deliberately delayed by 1 to 2 hours to lower the risk of cerebral oedema.

sources.label: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines - DKA

stem: A 7-month-old boy is brought to the paediatric ward where you are the registrar with eight days of fever to 39.5 degrees C, persistent irritability, and refusal of feeds. He has a maculopapular rash on his trunk and conjunctival injection without exudate. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy and his oral mucosa is unremarkable. His mother reports a brief peeling at the fingertips noted yesterday. He has been treated with two courses of oral amoxicillin without improvement. There is no respiratory distress and no organomegaly.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, BP 92/56 mmHg, RR 36, Temp 39.2 degrees C, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: BCG scar site erythematous, mild perineal desquamation, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: CRP 162 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/h, platelets 612 x10^9/L, albumin 28 g/L, ALT 88 U/L, sterile pyuria on urinalysis

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange urgent echocardiography and commence intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg plus high-dose aspirin
options.rationale: Infants under 12 months frequently present with incomplete Kawasaki phenotype and have the highest risk of coronary artery aneurysm. With prolonged fever, BCG site reactivation, sterile pyuria, hypoalbuminaemia and thrombocytosis, treatment must not wait for full clinical criteria. Echocardiography defines baseline coronary status while immunoglobulin reduces aneurysm risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Continue supportive care with paracetamol and reassess after a further 48 hours of observation
options.rationale: Coronary aneurysm risk rises sharply after day 10 of untreated fever in infants, and this child is on day 8 already. Watchful waiting in this high-risk demographic is dangerous when supportive laboratory features of incomplete Kawasaki are present. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Switch to intravenous ceftriaxone for occult bacterial infection and add doxycycline for atypical pathogens
options.rationale: Broad-spectrum antibiotics do not treat Kawasaki disease and delay definitive immunoglobulin therapy. Two courses of amoxicillin have already failed to defervesce; the laboratory pattern is inflammatory, not bacterial. Doxycycline is also contraindicated under 8 years for non-life-threatening indications. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg pulse therapy alone for inflammatory vasculitis
options.rationale: Corticosteroids may be added to immunoglobulin in selected high-risk Kawasaki cases but are not first-line monotherapy in Australia. Steroid-only treatment misses the proven aneurysm-protective effect of immunoglobulin. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid culture and commence empirical aciclovir intravenously
options.rationale: There are no features of meningoencephalitis, and the clinical and laboratory picture is dominated by systemic inflammation with mucocutaneous signs. Aciclovir and lumbar puncture do not address the leading diagnosis and delay immunoglobulin therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease must be considered in any infant with prolonged unexplained fever, even when only two or three classical features are present. Supportive laboratory criteria include CRP above 30, ESR above 40, platelets above 450, albumin below 30, ALT elevation, and sterile pyuria. BCG site reactivation is a highly suggestive sign in Australian infants. Treatment with immunoglobulin within 10 days of fever onset cuts coronary aneurysm rates from around 25% to under 5%.
key_points: Infants under 12 months have the highest coronary aneurysm risk and atypical phenotype
Five supportive labs convert a two-feature infant into a treatable incomplete Kawasaki case
Immunoglobulin 2 g/kg single infusion plus high-dose aspirin is the Australian standard
BCG site reactivation is a useful Australian-specific clinical clue
explanation.pearls: In an infant under 12 months with fever for 5 or more days and 2 to 3 classical features, immunoglobulin 2 g/kg should be given within the first 10 days of fever to bring coronary aneurysm risk from around 25% down to under 5%.

sources.label: AHA Scientific Statement - Diagnosis Treatment and Long-term Management of Kawasaki Disease

subtopic_detail: Severe early jaundice with positive DAT
stem: You are the paediatric registrar covering the postnatal ward. A term male infant born at 39+4 weeks by spontaneous vaginal delivery is now 28 hours old and clinically jaundiced to the lower abdomen. His mother is blood group O Rh-positive, with anti-c antibodies detected at 28 weeks at a titre of 1:32. Cord blood at delivery showed haemoglobin 138 g/L and direct antiglobulin test positive. The infant is feeding eagerly. Birth weight was 3450 g and weight today is 3320 g. The transcutaneous bilirubin reading is 290 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 148 bpm, RR 42, Temp 36.9 degrees C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, vigorous suck, no hepatosplenomegaly, scleral icterus to mid-trunk
data_table.Investigations: Serum total bilirubin 318 micromol/L, conjugated 8 micromol/L, Hb 132 g/L, reticulocytes 8%, blood group A Rh-positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with community midwife follow-up and a repeat bilirubin sample in 24 hours
options.rationale: Discharging a term infant with confirmed isoimmune haemolysis and bilirubin 318 micromol/L at 28 hours risks rapid kernicterus. The trajectory of antibody-driven haemolysis is unpredictable and demands inpatient management. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Commence intensive multi-light phototherapy and prepare for possible exchange transfusion at this level
options.rationale: Serum bilirubin of 318 micromol/L at 28 hours of age in a term infant with confirmed isoimmune haemolysis crosses the intensive phototherapy threshold on the Australian gestational-age-adjusted graph and approaches the exchange line. Anti-c is a clinically significant antibody causing haemolytic disease similar in pattern to anti-D. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Commence single-bank conventional phototherapy and recheck the bilirubin at 24 hours
options.rationale: Conventional single-light therapy is insufficient when bilirubin is approaching the exchange threshold with active haemolysis. Intensive multi-light phototherapy delivers more irradiance and is appropriate for high-risk haemolytic jaundice. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Administer oral phenobarbitone 5 mg/kg daily as a hepatic enzyme inducer and avoid phototherapy
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is not an accepted treatment for established neonatal haemolytic jaundice in Australia. The infant needs immediate light therapy and possibly exchange, not enzyme induction. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Withhold treatment and arrange repeat transcutaneous bilirubin in 6 hours before deciding on therapy
options.rationale: Once total serum bilirubin has confirmed a level crossing the phototherapy threshold in a high-risk infant, treatment must commence immediately. Transcutaneous values are unreliable above 250 micromol/L and after phototherapy starts. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

explanation.summary: Anti-c is a clinically significant non-D Rh antibody capable of causing severe haemolytic disease of the newborn. Cord DAT positivity, early jaundice within 24 to 36 hours, and rapid rise of bilirubin warrant intensive phototherapy and pre-emptive exchange preparation. The Australian phototherapy and exchange thresholds are age-in-hours and gestation-adjusted, with haemolysis lowering the trigger by approximately 35 micromol/L. Intravenous immunoglobulin 0.5 to 1 g/kg may also be considered as an adjunct in isoimmune haemolysis approaching exchange thresholds.
key_points: Anti-c can cause severe haemolytic disease equivalent in severity to anti-D
Phototherapy and exchange thresholds are lowered by approximately 35 micromol/L when haemolysis is confirmed
Intensive multi-light phototherapy delivers higher irradiance than single-bank
Intravenous immunoglobulin 0.5 to 1 g/kg can reduce the need for exchange in isoimmune cases
explanation.pearls: In confirmed isoimmune haemolytic jaundice approaching the exchange line, intravenous immunoglobulin 0.5 to 1 g/kg given alongside intensive phototherapy reduces the need for exchange transfusion by more than 50%.

sources.label: NICE NG98 - Jaundice in Newborn Babies under 28 Days
Australian Red Cross Lifeblood - Anti-c in Pregnancy

stem: A 6-week-old girl is brought to the emergency department where you are the registrar after a single episode at home where she became limp and pale for around 20 seconds during a feed before returning to her normal state. She has fed and behaved normally since. She was born at 38 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal delivery. There is no fever, no respiratory symptoms, no preceding illness, and no family history of sudden infant death. Examination on arrival is normal. Her parents are very anxious but the child appears completely well on your assessment.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142 bpm, RR 38, Temp 36.8 degrees C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused, normal tone, anterior fontanelle soft, chest clear, no rash, no bruising
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood glucose 4.6 mmol/L, paired pre-and-post-ductal saturations both 99%

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Perform a full septic screen including lumbar puncture and commence empirical intravenous antibiotics
options.rationale: A well infant without fever, lethargy, or focus does not require empiric sepsis treatment based on a single brief episode. Routine LP and broad antibiotics are not part of BRUE evaluation. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

options.text: Discharge home with shared follow-up by the general practitioner within 24 hours and infant CPR education
options.rationale: Lower-risk BRUE discharge applies only to infants over 60 days who meet all low-risk criteria. This 6-week-old infant does not qualify regardless of how reassuring the current examination appears. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

options.text: Admit for at least 4 hours of monitored observation with paired saturation checks before discharge planning
options.rationale: Although her episode meets brief resolved unexplained event criteria, she is under 60 days old, which automatically places her in the higher-risk category and warrants a period of monitored observation. A 4-hour observation period with cardiorespiratory monitoring is the recommended minimum in Australian paediatric emergency protocols. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

options.text: Arrange outpatient electroencephalography and discharge home pending the test result in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Outpatient EEG without admission is not appropriate for an under-60-day infant with an unexplained event. The yield of unprovoked EEG is low and observation in the higher-risk group should be inpatient. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

options.text: Arrange immediate outpatient 24-hour Holter monitoring and discharge home with cardiology phone advice only
options.rationale: Holter monitoring is not the standard first investigation for BRUE in the absence of suspected arrhythmia or family history of long QT. The age-based higher-risk classification mandates inpatient monitoring, not outpatient cardiology. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

explanation.summary: Brief resolved unexplained events are stratified into lower and higher risk based on age, gestation, episode features, and prior medical history. Lower-risk infants must be over 60 days, term, with a single episode under 1 minute, and no concerning history or examination. All infants under 60 days automatically fall into the higher-risk group regardless of how well they appear. Higher-risk infants warrant a period of monitored observation, paired saturations, glucose, ECG, and sometimes admission for further workup.
key_points: Age under 60 days is an automatic higher-risk criterion in BRUE
Lower-risk discharge requires age over 60 days, term gestation, single episode under 1 minute, normal exam, no prior BRUE
Higher-risk infants need monitored observation for at least 4 hours plus ECG and paired saturations
Family CPR education and clear safety-net advice are part of every BRUE disposition
explanation.pearls: Any infant under 60 days with a brief resolved unexplained event is automatically higher-risk by Australian and AAP definitions and needs at least 4 hours of monitored observation, regardless of how well they look in the emergency department.
